1:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Depart, and go up hence, thou and the people which thou hast brought up out of the land of Egypt, unto the land which I sware unto Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, saying, Unto thy seed will I give it:" The witnesses note God bidding Moses and the people depart for the land sworn to the fathers. Gill notes the command was to go up, not from the place where Moses was, the top of the mount, but where the people were, to depart toward Canaan. The witnesses note God commanding Moses and the people to depart and go up to the land sworn to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; the renewing of the march toward the promised land, the oath to the fathers held fast — that, despite the calf, God still owns his oath and sends them on: "Depart, and go up hence... unto the land which I sware unto Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob"; the witnesses note the command, "Depart, and go up hence" — that they should set forward from Sinai toward Canaan; the witnesses note the ground of the journey, "the land which I sware unto Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob" — that the promise still stood on the oath to the fathers, "Unto thy seed will I give it"; the witnesses note the comfort, that, though the people had sinned grievously, God held fast his ancient covenant and oath, for his promise fails not; the witnesses note that the faithfulness of God to his oath, not the worthiness of the people, carried them on; so that God sends them up to the land sworn to the fathers; Unto thy seed will I give it. The witnesses note And the LORD said unto Moses, Depart, and go up hence. The witnesses note unto the land which I sware unto Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, saying, Unto thy seed will I give it.

The witnesses note God bidding Moses and the people depart for the land sworn to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — owning his ancient oath despite the calf, though speaking of them with a certain distance as "the people which thou hast brought up out of Egypt"; the faithfulness of God to his oath, not the worthiness of the people, carrying them on.

So the witnesses make the verse the renewing of the march, the oath to the fathers held fast. The lesson the expositors draw is that God, despite the great sin, still owned his oath to the fathers and sent them toward Canaan, his faithfulness carrying them on. The witnesses note the depart and go up: "Depart, and go up hence" — Gill notes toward Canaan; the depart and go up; the toward Canaan; the from Sinai; the set forward; the the journey. The expositors observe the thou hast brought up: "the people which thou hast brought up" — a certain distance; the after their sin. The witnesses press the the oath: "the land which I sware" — to the fathers; the Abraham. The expositors draw the lesson on the the promise stands: the promise still stood; the the promise stands; the still stood; the the oath; the Unto thy seed; the I give it. The expositors note the fails not: God's promise fails not. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unto the land which I sware unto Abraham to Isaac and to Jacob saying Unto thy seed will I give it, and the march. The verse thus records the renewing of the march — teaching that God held fast his oath to the fathers, his faithfulness carrying them on.
2:
"And I will send an angel before thee; and I will drive out the Canaanite, the Amorite, and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite:" The witnesses note God promising to send an angel before them to drive out the nations. Gill notes the angel here was not the Angel before promised, the Angel of his presence, the eternal Word and Son of God, but a created angel. The witnesses note God promising to send an angel before Israel to drive out the Canaanite and the other nations; the offer of a created angel in place of God's own presence, a lesser favour after their sin — that God would send an angel, but not himself go up: "I will send an angel before thee; and I will drive out the Canaanite, the Amorite... the Jebusite"; the witnesses note the gift, "I will send an angel before thee" — but, as the commentators mark, "not the Angel before promised, the Angel of his presence, the eternal Word and Son of God, but a created angel"; the witnesses note the work promised, "I will drive out the Canaanite, the Amorite, and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite" — that the nations would still be driven out before them; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that God would still give them the land and a guardian, but the threatened loss of his own immediate presence was the sore part, a token of his displeasure; the witnesses note that to have all the gifts without the Giver's presence is a poor thing; so that God offers an angel, but not his own presence; the Hivite, and the Jebusite. The witnesses note And I will send an angel before thee. The witnesses note and I will drive out the Canaanite, the Amorite, and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite.

The witnesses note God promising to send an angel — not the Angel of his presence (the eternal Word), but a created angel — to drive out the nations; a lesser favour after the calf, the threatened loss of his own immediate presence the sore part, a token of his displeasure; to have all the gifts without the Giver's presence a poor thing.

So the witnesses make the verse the offer of a created angel in place of God's own presence. The lesson the expositors draw is that God would still drive out the nations and give the land, but the withholding of his own presence was the sore part, the gifts without the Giver poor. The witnesses note the send an angel: "I will send an angel" — Gill notes a created angel; the send an angel; the a created angel; the not the Angel of his presence; the not the Word; the a lesser thing. The expositors observe the the contrast: earlier the Angel of his name, now a created angel. The witnesses press the drive out the nations: "drive out the Canaanite... Jebusite" — the land still given; the drive out the nations; the the land; the still given; the the Canaanite; the the rest. The expositors draw the lesson on the the sore part: the loss of his presence; the the sore part; the the loss; the his presence; the displeasure; the the token. The expositors note the a poor thing: all without his presence a poor thing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and I will drive out the Canaanite the Amorite and the Hittite and the Perizzite the Hivite and the Jebusite, and the angel. The verse thus records the offer of a created angel — teaching that God would give the land but withhold his own presence, the gifts without the Giver poor.
3:
"Unto a land flowing with milk and honey: for I will not go up in the midst of thee; for thou art a stiffnecked people: lest I consume thee in the way." The witnesses note God refusing to go up in their midst, lest his holiness consume the stiffnecked people. Gill notes the land flowing with milk and honey, abounding with all the good things of life, a description of Canaan; but God would not go up in the midst of them. The witnesses note God promising the good land, but declaring he would not go up in their midst, for they were stiffnecked, lest he consume them; the sore reason for withholding his presence, the danger of the holy God dwelling among a sinful people — that his very presence might destroy them: "I will not go up in the midst of thee; for thou art a stiffnecked people: lest I consume thee in the way"; the witnesses note the land, "a land flowing with milk and honey" — Canaan, "abounding with all the necessaries and good things of life"; the witnesses note the refusal, "I will not go up in the midst of thee" — that God would not go among them as before, in his immediate presence; the witnesses note the reason, "for thou art a stiffnecked people: lest I consume thee in the way" — that, being so prone to sin and provoking, the holy God dwelling among them might break out in just wrath and consume them; the witnesses note that it is mercy as well as judgment that God withdraws, sparing them; the witnesses note that only through a Mediator can the holy God safely dwell with sinners; so that God withholds his presence, lest it consume them; lest I consume thee in the way. The witnesses note Unto a land flowing with milk and honey: for I will not go up in the midst of thee. The witnesses note for thou art a stiffnecked people: lest I consume thee in the way.

The witnesses note God promising the good land but refusing to go up in their midst, lest his holiness consume the stiffnecked people — the nearer presence of the holy God to a sinful people the more dangerous, his holiness a consuming fire to sin; it being mercy as well as judgment that he withdraws, only through a Mediator can the holy God safely dwell with sinners.

So the witnesses make the verse the sore reason for withholding his presence. The lesson the expositors draw is that God would not go in their midst lest he consume them, his holiness a consuming fire to sin, only through a Mediator can the holy God dwell with sinners. The witnesses note the the land: "a land flowing with milk and honey" — Gill notes Canaan; the the land; the Canaan; the the good things; the abounding; the the necessaries. The expositors observe the not in the midst: "I will not go up in the midst" — not as before; the withheld. The witnesses press the stiffnecked: "thou art a stiffnecked people" — prone to sin; the stiffnecked; the prone to sin; the provoking; the the people; the so given. The expositors draw the lesson on the lest I consume thee: "lest I consume thee" — his wrath; the lest I consume thee; the his wrath; the consume; the the holy God; the break out. The expositors note the through a Mediator: only through a Mediator can the holy God dwell with sinners. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for thou art a stiffnecked people lest I consume thee in the way, and the reason. The verse thus records the withholding of his presence — teaching that God would not go in their midst lest he consume them, only through a Mediator can the holy God dwell with sinners.
4:
"And when the people heard these evil tidings, they mourned: and no man did put on him his ornaments." The witnesses note the people mourning at the evil tidings, putting off their ornaments. Gill notes the evil tidings were that God would withdraw his gracious presence and not go up with them himself, only sending an angel; at this they mourned. The witnesses note the people, hearing these evil tidings, mourning and putting off their ornaments; the people's grief at the threatened loss of God's presence, a hopeful sign of repentance — that the worst news was the withdrawing of God himself: "when the people heard these evil tidings, they mourned: and no man did put on him his ornaments"; the witnesses note the tidings, "these evil tidings" — namely, that "God would withdraw his gracious presence, and go not up with them himself, only send" an angel; the witnesses note the grief, "they mourned" — that the people were grieved and cast down at this word, the loss of God's presence accounted the heaviest of evils; the witnesses note the token of mourning, "no man did put on him his ornaments" — that they laid aside their finery, in humiliation and sorrow, as mourners; the witnesses note the hopeful sign the commentators draw, that the people's grief was a token of true sorrow and beginning repentance, that they so felt the loss of God; the witnesses note that to a gracious heart the withdrawing of God's presence is the sorest of all losses; the witnesses note that the loss of God is felt by those who love him above all gifts; so that the people mourn and strip their ornaments; and no man did put on him his ornaments. The witnesses note And when the people heard these evil tidings, they mourned. The witnesses note and no man did put on him his ornaments.

The witnesses note the people mourning and putting off their ornaments at the evil tidings that God would withdraw his presence — the loss of God's presence accounted the heaviest of evils; a hopeful sign of true sorrow and beginning repentance, for to a gracious heart the withdrawing of God's presence is the sorest of all losses.

So the witnesses make the verse the people's grief at the threatened loss of God's presence. The lesson the expositors draw is that the people mourned and stripped their ornaments, the loss of God the heaviest of evils, a hopeful sign of repentance. The witnesses note the evil tidings: "these evil tidings" — Gill notes God withdrawing; the evil tidings; the God withdrawing; the his presence; the only an angel; the not himself. The expositors observe the they mourned: "they mourned" — grieved; the they mourned; the grieved; the cast down; the the heaviest; the of evils. The witnesses press the put off ornaments: "no man did put on... ornaments" — humiliation; the put off ornaments; the humiliation; the laid aside; the finery; the mourners. The expositors draw the lesson on the a hopeful sign: a token of repentance; the a hopeful sign; the a token; the repentance; the true sorrow; the beginning. The witnesses observe the sorest loss: the withdrawing of God the sorest; the sorest loss; the the withdrawing; the the sorest; the of losses; the gracious heart. The expositors note the felt by those who love him: felt by those who love him; the felt by those; the who love him; the above all; the gifts; the loss of God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And when the people heard these evil tidings they mourned, the and no man did put on him his ornaments, and the grief. The verse thus records the people's grief — teaching that they mourned the loss of God's presence, the heaviest of evils, a hopeful sign of repentance.
5:
"For the LORD had said unto Moses, Say unto the children of Israel, Ye are a stiffnecked people: I will come up into the midst of thee in a moment, and consume thee: therefore now put off thy ornaments from thee, that I may know what to do unto thee." The witnesses note God bidding them put off their ornaments, that he might know what to do with them. Keil and Delitzsch note the LORD bade Israel put off their ornaments, as a sign of humiliation, that he might consider how to deal with them. The witnesses note God charging Israel, as a stiffnecked people whom his presence might consume, to put off their ornaments, that he might know what to do with them; the call to humiliation, that mercy might find a way — that they must humble themselves and lay aside their pride: "Ye are a stiffnecked people... therefore now put off thy ornaments from thee, that I may know what to do unto thee"; the witnesses note the charge, "Ye are a stiffnecked people" — the same hard truth pressed home, the cause of all the danger; the witnesses note the peril, "I will come up into the midst of thee in a moment, and consume thee" — that his holy presence, among such a people, might in a moment break out and destroy them; the witnesses note the command, "put off thy ornaments from thee" — that they should lay aside their finery, "a sign of humiliation," abasing themselves before God; the witnesses note that God calls the sinner to humble himself, that mercy may find a way; the witnesses note that humiliation goes before grace; so that they put off their ornaments in humiliation; that I may know what to do unto thee. The witnesses note Ye are a stiffnecked people: I will come up into the midst of thee in a moment, and consume thee. The witnesses note therefore now put off thy ornaments from thee, that I may know what to do unto thee.

The witnesses note God charging the stiffnecked people, whom his presence might in a moment consume, to put off their ornaments in humiliation, that he might know what to do with them — spoken after the manner of men, leaving room for mercy on their repentance; God calling the sinner to humble himself, that mercy may find a way, humiliation going before grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to humiliation, that mercy might find a way. The lesson the expositors draw is that God bade them put off their ornaments and abase themselves, that mercy might have room on their repentance, humiliation going before grace. The witnesses note the stiffnecked: "Ye are a stiffnecked people" — Keil notes the cause of danger; the stiffnecked; the the cause; the of danger; the pressed home; the hard truth. The expositors observe the consume thee: "consume thee in a moment" — his presence; the break out. The witnesses press the put off ornaments: "put off thy ornaments" — humiliation; the put off ornaments; the humiliation; the abase; the the finery; the laid aside. The expositors draw the lesson on the what to do: "that I may know what to do" — leaving room for mercy; the what to do; the room for mercy; the their repentance; the after the manner of men; the consider. The expositors note the before grace: humiliation goes before grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the therefore now put off thy ornaments from thee that I may know what to do unto thee, and the humiliation. The verse thus records the call to humiliation — teaching that God bade them abase themselves, that mercy might find a way, humiliation going before grace.
6:
"And the children of Israel stripped themselves of their ornaments by the mount Horeb." The witnesses note the children of Israel stripping off their ornaments by Mount Horeb. Keil and Delitzsch note the children of Israel stripped themselves of their ornaments from Mount Horeb onward, putting them off as a lasting token of penitence. The witnesses note the children of Israel stripping themselves of their ornaments by Mount Horeb; the people's obedience in humiliation, the outward token of their penitence — that they did as God bade, laying aside their finery: "the children of Israel stripped themselves of their ornaments by the mount Horeb"; the witnesses note the act, "stripped themselves of their ornaments" — that they obeyed the command, putting off "such as before described," the finery they had worn; the witnesses note the place and time, "by the mount Horeb" — there at Sinai, and "from Mount Horeb onward," as a lasting token; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that this stripping was the outward sign of inward humiliation and penitence, the people abasing themselves before the God they had offended; the witnesses note that true repentance shows itself in humbling acts, the laying aside of pride and ornament; the witnesses note that the people who had so lately decked themselves and sinned now stripped themselves and mourned; the witnesses note that humiliation is the fit posture of a people that has provoked God; so that Israel strips off its ornaments in penitence; by the mount Horeb. The witnesses note And the children of Israel stripped themselves of their ornaments. The witnesses note by the mount Horeb.

The witnesses note Israel stripping off their ornaments by Mount Horeb in obedience — the outward token of inward humiliation and penitence, the people abasing themselves before the God they had offended; true repentance showing itself in humbling acts, the laying aside of pride and ornament.

So the witnesses make the verse the people's obedience in humiliation. The lesson the expositors draw is that Israel stripped off their ornaments in penitence, the outward sign of inward humiliation, true repentance showing itself in humbling acts. The witnesses note the stripped themselves: "stripped themselves of their ornaments" — Keil notes a lasting token; the stripped themselves; the a lasting token; the obeyed; the the finery; the put off. The expositors observe the by Horeb: "by the mount Horeb" — at Sinai; the by Horeb; the at Sinai; the from Horeb onward; the there; the the place. The witnesses press the inward humiliation: the sign of inward humiliation; the inward humiliation; the the sign; the inward; the humiliation; the penitence. The expositors draw the lesson on the abasing themselves: abasing themselves before God; the abasing themselves; the abasing; the before God; the offended; the the people. The witnesses observe the humbling acts: repentance in humbling acts; the humbling acts; the repentance; the humbling; the acts; the laid aside. The expositors note the fit posture: humiliation the fit posture; the fit posture; the the fit; the posture; the provoked God; the the people. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And the children of Israel stripped themselves of their ornaments, the by the mount Horeb, and the humiliation. The verse thus records the stripping of ornaments — teaching that Israel humbled themselves in penitence, true repentance showing itself in humbling acts.
7:
"And Moses took the tabernacle, and pitched it without the camp, afar off from the camp... And it came to pass, that every one which sought the LORD went out unto the tabernacle of the congregation, which was without the camp." The witnesses note Moses pitching the tabernacle outside the camp, whither all who sought the LORD went out. Gill notes this was not the great tabernacle, the pattern of which had been shown, for that was not yet made, but Moses' own tent, or one erected for worship while the great one was building. The witnesses note Moses taking the tabernacle and pitching it far outside the camp, where all who sought the LORD went out to it; the removing of the place of meeting outside the camp, the distance sin had made — that, sin having grieved God, the meeting-place is set afar off: "Moses took the tabernacle, and pitched it without the camp... every one which sought the LORD went out unto the tabernacle... which was without the camp"; the witnesses note the tent, "the tabernacle" — not the great tabernacle (not yet built), but "his own tabernacle or tent, or one... erected for worship" in the meantime; the witnesses note the place, "without the camp, afar off from the camp" — that, after the calf, the place of meeting with God was removed outside the camp, a token that sin had put a distance between God and the people; the witnesses note the seekers, "every one which sought the LORD went out unto the tabernacle" — that those who truly sought God had to go forth, outside the polluted camp, to seek him; the witnesses note that the way to God was now outside the camp, a place of separation; so that the meeting-place is set outside the camp, sought by the seekers; which was without the camp. The witnesses note Moses took the tabernacle, and pitched it without the camp, afar off from the camp. The witnesses note every one which sought the LORD went out unto the tabernacle of the congregation, which was without the camp.

The witnesses note Moses pitching the tent of meeting far outside the camp, whither all who sought the LORD went out — a token that sin had put a distance between God and the people; sin separating from God and removing his presence, those who would find him going forth to seek him, even bearing reproach, the way to God now outside the camp.

So the witnesses make the verse the removing of the meeting-place outside the camp. The lesson the expositors draw is that sin had put a distance, the place of meeting set afar off, those who sought God going forth to seek him outside the polluted camp. The witnesses note the the tabernacle: "the tabernacle" — Gill notes not the great one; the the tabernacle; the not the great one; the his own tent; the for worship; the meanwhile. The expositors observe the without the camp: "without the camp, afar off" — the distance; the without the camp; the the distance; the sin had made; the removed; the a token. The witnesses press the the seekers: "every one which sought the LORD went out" — forth; the outside. The expositors draw the lesson on the sin separates: sin separates from God; the sin separates; the sin; the separates; the removes; the his presence. The expositors note the the way outside: the way to God now outside the camp. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the every one which sought the LORD went out unto the tabernacle of the congregation which was without the camp, and the meeting-place. The verse thus records the removing of the meeting-place — teaching that sin had put a distance, those who sought God going forth outside the camp.
8:
"And it came to pass, when Moses went out unto the tabernacle, that all the people rose up, and stood every man at his tent door, and looked after Moses, until he was gone into the tabernacle." The witnesses note the people rising and standing at their tent doors, looking after Moses as he went to the tabernacle. Gill notes that when Moses had pitched it he did not continue there, but went to and fro, to meet the Lord and to transact the affairs of the people, especially the great affair of their reconciliation. The witnesses note the people rising and standing at their tent doors, looking after Moses as he went out to the tabernacle; the reverent regard of the people for their mediator going to God — that they watch Moses go in to plead for them: "when Moses went out unto the tabernacle... all the people rose up, and stood every man at his tent door, and looked after Moses"; the witnesses note the going forth, "when Moses went out unto the tabernacle" — that Moses "went to and fro," not dwelling there but going "to meet the Lord... and especially the great affair" of the people's reconciliation; the witnesses note the people's posture, "all the people rose up, and stood every man at his tent door" — that, in reverence, they all rose and stood, watching; the witnesses note the looking, "looked after Moses, until he was gone into the tabernacle" — that they followed him with their eyes till he entered, anxious for the outcome of his pleading; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the people, conscious of their sin and danger, looked to their mediator with reverence and hope, knowing all depended on his meeting with God; the witnesses note that all our hope hangs on our Mediator's going in to God; so that the people watch their mediator go in to plead; until he was gone into the tabernacle. The witnesses note when Moses went out unto the tabernacle, all the people rose up, and stood every man at his tent door. The witnesses note and looked after Moses, until he was gone into the tabernacle.

The witnesses note the people rising and standing at their tent doors, looking after Moses as he went to plead for them — the reverent regard of a people conscious of their sin for their mediator; as Israel looked after Moses, so sinners look to Christ, the great Mediator, gone in to God for them, all our hope hanging on his going in.

So the witnesses make the verse the reverent regard of the people for their mediator. The lesson the expositors draw is that the people, conscious of their sin, watched Moses go in to plead, knowing all depended on his meeting with God, a type of looking to Christ the Mediator. The witnesses note the went out: "when Moses went out" — Gill notes to and fro; the went out; the to and fro; the to meet the Lord; the the affairs; the reconciliation. The expositors observe the rose up: "all the people rose up" — in reverence; the watching. The witnesses press the looked after: "looked after Moses" — anxious; the his pleading. The expositors draw the lesson on the conscious of sin: conscious of their sin; the conscious of sin; the conscious; the their sin; the danger; the their mediator. The expositors note the hope on the Mediator: all our hope hangs on the Mediator going in. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and looked after Moses until he was gone into the tabernacle, and the regard. The verse thus records the reverent regard of the people — teaching that they watched their mediator go in to plead, a type of looking to Christ the Mediator.
9:
"And it came to pass, as Moses entered into the tabernacle, the cloudy pillar descended, and stood at the door of the tabernacle, and the LORD talked with Moses." The witnesses note the cloudy pillar descending and standing at the door as the LORD talked with Moses. Adam Clarke notes that the cloud descending at these times, and at none others, precluded the possibility of deception, a full proof of the divine presence. The witnesses note that, as Moses entered the tabernacle, the cloudy pillar descended and stood at the door, and the LORD talked with him; the visible token of God's presence and his speaking with Moses — that the cloud comes down where the mediator enters, and God speaks: "as Moses entered into the tabernacle, the cloudy pillar descended, and stood at the door of the tabernacle, and the LORD talked with Moses"; the witnesses note the descent, "the cloudy pillar descended" — coming down "from the top of the mount in which Jehovah was," the visible sign of God's presence; the witnesses note the standing, "stood at the door of the tabernacle" — that it rested at the door where Moses was, marking the place of meeting; the witnesses note the proof Clarke draws, that "the cloud descending at these times, and at none others, was a full" proof, "preclud[ing] the possibility of deception," that God indeed met with Moses; the witnesses note the speaking, "the LORD talked with Moses" — that God conversed with him there; the witnesses note the meaning, that God answers the mediator who goes in for the people, coming down to meet and speak with him; the witnesses note the comfort, that, though removed from the camp, God still met with Moses for the people's sake; the witnesses note that God hears and answers the intercessor; so that the cloud descends and God talks with Moses; and the LORD talked with Moses. The witnesses note as Moses entered into the tabernacle, the cloudy pillar descended, and stood at the door of the tabernacle. The witnesses note and the LORD talked with Moses.

The witnesses note the cloudy pillar descending and standing at the door as the LORD talked with Moses — the visible token of God's presence (the cloud coming at these times and none others, a full proof against deception); God answering the mediator who goes in for the people, still meeting Moses for their sake though removed from the camp.

So the witnesses make the verse the visible token of God's presence and his speaking with Moses. The lesson the expositors draw is that the cloud came down where the mediator entered and God talked with him, a proof of his presence, God answering the intercessor for the people. The witnesses note the cloudy pillar: "the cloudy pillar descended" — Clarke notes from the mount; the cloudy pillar; the from the mount; the the presence; the the sign; the came down. The expositors observe the stood at the door: "stood at the door" — the place of meeting; the stood at the door; the the place of meeting; the rested; the where Moses was; the marked. The witnesses press the proof against deception: a full proof, no deception; the at these times and none others. The expositors draw the lesson on the the LORD talked: "the LORD talked with Moses" — conversed; the the LORD talked; the conversed; the with Moses; the spake; the there. The expositors note the met for their sake: still met for their sake, though removed from the camp. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and the LORD talked with Moses, and the presence. The verse thus records the token of God's presence — teaching that the cloud came down and God talked with Moses, God answering the intercessor for the people.
10:
"And all the people saw the cloudy pillar stand at the tabernacle door: and all the people rose up and worshipped, every man in his tent door." The witnesses note all the people seeing the cloudy pillar and rising up to worship at their tent doors. John Wesley notes that when they saw the cloudy pillar, they worshipped, thereby owning God's presence with Moses and giving him their hearts and reverence. The witnesses note all the people, seeing the cloudy pillar stand at the door, rising up and worshipping at their tent doors; the people's worship at the sight of God's presence, a hopeful token of mercy — that they bow at the sign of God still near: "all the people saw the cloudy pillar stand at the tabernacle door: and all the people rose up and worshipped, every man in his tent door"; the witnesses note the sight, "all the people saw the cloudy pillar stand at the tabernacle door" — the visible token that God was still meeting with Moses; the witnesses note the comfort, that this was "a pleasing sight to them," giving "some hope that God would be merciful to them, forgive their sin, and not depart from them"; the witnesses note the worship, "all the people rose up and worshipped, every man in his tent door" — that, owning God's presence with Moses, they bowed in reverence and adoration; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the sight of God's presence draws forth worship, and that the people, hoping in his mercy through the mediator, worshipped; the witnesses note that those who hope in God's mercy through the Mediator worship him; the witnesses note that the token of God's nearness is a call to worship; so that the people worship at the sight of God's presence; every man in his tent door. The witnesses note all the people saw the cloudy pillar stand at the tabernacle door. The witnesses note and all the people rose up and worshipped, every man in his tent door.

The witnesses note all the people rising up to worship at the sight of the cloudy pillar — owning God's presence with Moses, a pleasing sight that gave them hope of his mercy and forgiveness; the sight of God's presence drawing forth worship, those who hope in his mercy through the Mediator worshipping him.

So the witnesses make the verse the people's worship at the sight of God's presence. The lesson the expositors draw is that the people worshipped at the cloudy pillar, hoping in God's mercy through the mediator, the sight of his presence drawing forth worship. The witnesses note the saw the pillar: "saw the cloudy pillar" — Wesley notes owning God's presence; the saw the pillar; the owning; the God's presence; the with Moses; the the sight. The expositors observe the a pleasing sight: "a pleasing sight" — hope of mercy; the a pleasing sight; the hope of mercy; the forgive; the not depart; the comfort. The witnesses press the rose up and worshipped: "rose up and worshipped" — bowed; the rose up and worshipped; the bowed; the reverence; the adoration; the their tent doors. The expositors draw the lesson on the draws worship: the sight draws worship; the draws worship; the the sight; the draws; the worship; the his presence. The expositors note the a call to worship: his nearness a call to worship. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and all the people rose up and worshipped every man in his tent door, and the worship. The verse thus records the people's worship — teaching that they bowed at the sight of God's presence, hoping in his mercy through the mediator.
11:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses face to face, as a man speaketh unto his friend. And he turned again into the camp: but his servant Joshua, the son of Nun, a young man, departed not out of the tabernacle." The witnesses note the LORD speaking to Moses face to face as a friend, and Joshua remaining in the tabernacle. Gill notes the LORD spake to Moses not by an angel but himself in person, not by dream or vision but apparently, in real visible appearance, clearly, mouth to mouth. The witnesses note the LORD speaking with Moses face to face, as a man with his friend, Moses returning to the camp but Joshua not departing from the tabernacle; the high privilege of Moses' communion with God, and the faithful waiting of Joshua — that God speaks with Moses as a friend, plainly and intimately: "the LORD spake unto Moses face to face, as a man speaketh unto his friend"; the witnesses note the manner, "face to face... as a man speaketh unto his friend" — "not by an angel, but he himself in person; not by a dream or vision, but apparently... clearly... mouth to mouth," with the openness and intimacy of friendship; the witnesses note the rare honour, that this was a privilege above the prophets, who had dreams and dark speeches (Num. 12:6-8), Moses speaking with God as a friend; the witnesses note the type some draw, that this intimate communion shadows the fellowship believers have with God in Christ, who calls them friends (John 15:15); the witnesses note Moses' return, "he turned again into the camp" — going back to the people with God's word; the witnesses note Joshua's waiting, "his servant Joshua... departed not out of the tabernacle" — that the young man kept by the place of God's presence, faithful and waiting; so that God speaks with Moses as a friend, and Joshua waits; departed not out of the tabernacle. The witnesses note the LORD spake unto Moses face to face, as a man speaketh unto his friend. The witnesses note but his servant Joshua, the son of Nun, a young man, departed not out of the tabernacle.

The witnesses note the LORD speaking with Moses face to face, as a man with his friend — not by dream or vision but plainly, mouth to mouth, a privilege above the prophets; some seeing it shadow the fellowship believers have with God in Christ, who calls them friends; and Joshua, the young man, keeping faithfully by the place of God's presence.

So the witnesses make the verse the high privilege of Moses' communion and Joshua's faithful waiting. The lesson the expositors draw is that God spoke with Moses as a friend, plainly and intimately, above the prophets, and Joshua kept by the tabernacle, faithful. The witnesses note the face to face: "face to face... as a man... unto his friend" — Gill notes in person; the face to face; the in person; the not an angel; the not a vision; the mouth to mouth. The expositors observe the the openness: the openness of friendship; the the openness; the of friendship; the clearly; the intimately; the plainly. The witnesses press the above the prophets: a privilege above the prophets; the dark speeches. The expositors draw the lesson on the fellowship in Christ: shadows fellowship in Christ; the fellowship in Christ; the fellowship; the in Christ; the calls them friends; the believers. The expositors note the Joshua waited: "Joshua... departed not" — kept by, faithful. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but his servant Joshua the son of Nun a young man departed not out of the tabernacle, and the communion. The verse thus records the privilege of Moses' communion — teaching that God spoke with him as a friend, plainly, and Joshua kept faithfully by the tabernacle.
12:
"And Moses said unto the LORD, See, thou sayest unto me, Bring up this people: and thou hast not let me know whom thou wilt send with me. Yet thou hast said, I know thee by name, and thou hast also found grace in my sight." The witnesses note Moses pleading with God, urging that he was bidden to bring up the people yet not told who should go with him. Matthew Henry notes that Moses, returned to the door of the tabernacle, becomes a humble and importunate supplicant for two great favours, and as a prince has power with God and prevails. The witnesses note Moses laying his case before God, that he was bidden to bring up the people but not told who should go with him, pleading God's word that he knew him by name and he had found grace; the opening of Moses' intercession, pleading God's own promises — that he urges God's words back upon him: "thou sayest unto me, Bring up this people: and thou hast not let me know whom thou wilt send with me. Yet thou hast said, I know thee by name"; the witnesses note the case, "thou sayest... Bring up this people... and thou hast not let me know whom thou wilt send with me" — that the charge was laid on him without the promised presence; the witnesses note the plea, "thou hast said, I know thee by name, and thou hast also found grace in my sight" — that Moses pleads God's own gracious words, his being known by name and his finding grace, as the ground of his asking; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the way to prevail with God is to plead his own words and promises back to him; the witnesses note that God loves to be held to his word by an importunate faith; so that Moses pleads God's own promise in intercession; thou hast also found grace in my sight. The witnesses note thou sayest unto me, Bring up this people: and thou hast not let me know whom thou wilt send with me. The witnesses note Yet thou hast said, I know thee by name, and thou hast also found grace in my sight.

The witnesses note Moses opening his intercession, urging that he was bidden to bring up the people but not told who should go with him, and pleading God's own words — that he knew him by name and he had found grace; the way to prevail with God being to plead his own promises back to him, an importunate faith holding God to his word.

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of Moses' intercession, pleading God's own promises. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses, an importunate supplicant, urged God's own gracious words back upon him, the way to prevail being to plead his promises. The witnesses note the bring up this people: "Bring up this people" — Henry notes the charge laid on him; the bring up this people; the the charge; the laid on him; the without the presence; the bidden. The expositors observe the not let me know: "thou hast not let me know whom" — no promised presence; the untold. The witnesses press the I know thee by name: "I know thee by name" — God's word; the pleaded. The expositors note the importunate faith: held by importunate faith. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Yet thou hast said I know thee by name and thou hast also found grace in my sight, and the plea. The verse thus records the opening of Moses' intercession — teaching that he pleaded God's own words, the way to prevail being to plead his promises.
13:
"Now therefore, I pray thee, if I have found grace in thy sight, shew me now thy way, that I may know thee, that I may find grace in thy sight: and consider that this nation is thy people." The witnesses note Moses praying to be shown God's way, that he might know him, and pleading that the nation is God's people. Keil and Delitzsch note Moses prayed, if he had found grace, that God would show him his way, that he might know him and continue to find grace, and that God would consider the nation his people. The witnesses note Moses, on the ground of grace found, praying to be shown God's way, that he might know him and find grace, and pleading that this nation is God's people; the prayer to know God and the plea for the people — that Moses asks to know God's ways and owns the people as God's: "shew me now thy way, that I may know thee... and consider that this nation is thy people"; the witnesses note the ground, "if I have found grace in thy sight" — which Moses says "not as doubting... but as taking it for granted... and so argues from it," improving his interest in grace; the witnesses note the request, "shew me now thy way, that I may know thee, that I may find grace" — that Moses desired to know God's way and mind, to know him more, and so continue in his favour; the witnesses note the plea for the people, "consider that this nation is thy people" — that Moses, in his own asking, does not forget the people, but pleads that they are God's own, not to be cast off; the witnesses note that the true intercessor pleads for the people as God's; so that Moses asks to know God and pleads for the people; consider that this nation is thy people. The witnesses note shew me now thy way, that I may know thee, that I may find grace in thy sight. The witnesses note and consider that this nation is thy people.

The witnesses note Moses, on the ground of grace found, praying "shew me now thy way, that I may know thee" — to know God being the height of the believer's desire and the way to grow in grace — and pleading "consider that this nation is thy people," the true intercessor not forgetting the people but pleading that they are God's own.

So the witnesses make the verse the prayer to know God and the plea for the people. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses asked to know God's way, that he might know him and grow in grace, and pleaded that the nation was God's own. The witnesses note the if I have found grace: "if I have found grace" — Keil notes not doubting; the if I have found grace; the not doubting; the taken for granted; the argues from it; the his interest. The expositors observe the shew me thy way: "shew me now thy way" — to know him; the continue in grace. The witnesses press the to know thee: "that I may know thee" — the height of desire; the to grow in grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the this nation thy people: "this nation is thy people" — God's own; the this nation thy people; the God's own; the not cast off; the pleads; the the people. The expositors note the not forget them: does not forget the people. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and consider that this nation is thy people, and the prayer. The verse thus records the prayer to know God — teaching that Moses asked to know God's way and pleaded that the nation was God's own.
14:
"And he said, My presence shall go with thee, and I will give thee rest." The witnesses note God promising that his presence should go with Moses and give him rest. Adam Clarke notes the Hebrew, "my faces shall go," that God would give manifestations of his grace and goodness through the whole journey, varying his appearances as their need required. The witnesses note God answering that his presence should go with Moses and he would give him rest; the gracious granting of the very thing most desired, God's own presence — that God promises to go himself and give rest: "My presence shall go with thee, and I will give thee rest"; the witnesses note the promise, "My presence shall go with thee" — in the Hebrew "my faces shall go," that God himself, not a created angel, would go, giving "manifestations of his grace and goodness through the whole... journey," varying his appearances as their "necessities" required; the witnesses note the rest, "I will give thee rest" — both the rest of Canaan at the journey's end and the rest of soul that God's presence gives by the way; the witnesses note the great mercy the commentators draw, that God, entreated, grants his own presence, the very favour withheld after the calf, restoring what sin had forfeited; the witnesses note that God's presence is the soul's true rest, and that where he goes there is rest; the witnesses note that the answer of prayer is often more than was asked; so that God promises his presence and rest; and I will give thee rest. The witnesses note And he said, My presence shall go with thee. The witnesses note and I will give thee rest.

The witnesses note God granting the very thing most desired — "My presence shall go with thee" ("my faces shall go," varying his appearances as their need required) — "and I will give thee rest," both the rest of Canaan and the rest of soul his presence gives; God, entreated, restoring what sin had forfeited, his presence the soul's true rest.

So the witnesses make the verse the gracious granting of God's own presence. The lesson the expositors draw is that God promised to go himself and give rest, restoring the favour withheld after the calf, his presence the soul's true rest. The witnesses note the my presence: "My presence shall go" — Clarke notes my faces shall go; the my presence; the my faces shall go; the himself; the not an angel; the his grace. The expositors observe the manifestations: "manifestations of his grace" — varying as needed; the manifestations; the of his grace; the varying; the as needed; the the journey. The witnesses press the give thee rest: "I will give thee rest" — Canaan and soul; the give thee rest; the Canaan; the and soul; the by the way; the the end. The expositors draw the lesson on the restores the favour: restores what sin forfeited; the restores the favour; the restores; the what sin forfeited; the entreated; the granted. The witnesses observe the his presence rest: his presence the soul's rest; the his presence rest; the his presence; the the soul's rest; the where he goes; the there is rest. The expositors note the more than asked: the answer more than asked; the more than asked; the the answer; the more; the than asked; the of prayer. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And he said My presence shall go with thee, the and I will give thee rest, and the promise. The verse thus records the granting of God's presence — teaching that God promised to go himself and give rest, his presence the soul's true rest.
15:
"And he said unto him, If thy presence go not with me, carry us not up hence." The witnesses note Moses declaring that, if God's presence go not, they would not go up. Adam Clarke notes the Hebrew, "if thy faces do not go," that without supernatural assistance and the manifestations of God's peculiar providence and grace, they should not be carried up. The witnesses note Moses answering that, if God's presence went not with them, he would not have them carried up; the heart that prefers God's presence to all the gifts of the land — that without God's presence Moses will not stir: "If thy presence go not with me, carry us not up hence"; the witnesses note the resolve, "if thy presence go not with me" — in the Hebrew "if thy faces do not go," that without "the manifestations of [God's] peculiar providence and grace," and "supernatural assistance," Moses would not go on; the witnesses note the refusal, "carry us not up hence" — that he would rather stay in the wilderness with God than go to Canaan without him; the witnesses note the great lesson the commentators draw, that the believer values God's presence above all his gifts, and would rather have God in the desert than the land without him; the witnesses note that the presence of God is better than the promised land, the Giver better than the gift; the witnesses note that a gracious soul cannot be content with anything short of God himself; so that Moses will not go up without God's presence; carry us not up hence. The witnesses note And he said unto him, If thy presence go not with me. The witnesses note carry us not up hence.

The witnesses note Moses declaring that without God's presence he would not be carried up — preferring God in the wilderness to Canaan without him; the believer valuing God's presence above all his gifts, the presence of God better than the promised land, the Giver better than the gift, a gracious soul content with nothing short of God himself.

So the witnesses make the verse the heart that prefers God's presence to all. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses would rather stay with God than go without him, the believer valuing God's presence above all gifts, the Giver better than the gift. The witnesses note the if thy presence go not: "if thy presence go not" — Clarke notes if thy faces do not go; the if thy presence go not; the if thy faces; the do not go; the without his grace; the supernatural. The expositors observe the carry us not up: "carry us not up hence" — would not stir; the carry us not up; the would not stir; the rather stay; the the wilderness; the with God. The witnesses press the above all gifts: God's presence above all gifts; the above all gifts; the above; the all gifts; the the believer; the values. The expositors draw the lesson on the better than the land: presence better than the land; the better than the land; the presence; the the land; the the Giver; the the gift. The witnesses observe the nothing short of God: content with nothing short of God; the nothing short of God; the nothing; the short of God; the himself; the a gracious soul. The expositors note the rather God in the desert: rather God in the desert; the rather God; the in the desert; the than the land; the without him; the prefers. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And he said unto him If thy presence go not with me, the carry us not up hence, and the resolve. The verse thus records the heart that prefers God's presence — teaching that Moses would rather stay with God than go without him, the Giver better than the gift.
16:
"For wherein shall it be known here that I and thy people have found grace in thy sight? is it not in that thou goest with us? so shall we be separated, I and thy people, from all the people that are upon the face of the earth." The witnesses note Moses pleading that God's going with them is the only proof of grace and what separates them from all peoples. Adam Clarke notes that by this divine protection they should be saved from idolatry and preserved in the true worship of God, and so be separated from all the people on earth. The witnesses note Moses pleading that nothing but God's going with them could show that they had found grace, and that this alone separated them from all other peoples; God's presence the one mark and proof of his special favour — that his going with them is what distinguishes them: "wherein shall it be known... that I and thy people have found grace... is it not in that thou goest with us? so shall we be separated... from all the people that are upon the face of the earth"; the witnesses note the question, "wherein shall it be known... that I and thy people have found grace" — that there could be no other token of God's special favour; the witnesses note the answer, "is it not in that thou goest with us?" — that God's going with them was the one proof and mark of grace; the witnesses note the separation, "so shall we be separated... from all the people... upon the face of the earth" — that, by God's presence and protection, they should be "saved from idolatry... preserved in the true worship," and so set apart from all the nations; the witnesses note that what makes God's people peculiar is his dwelling among them; so that God's presence is the mark that separates his people; from all the people that are upon the face of the earth. The witnesses note wherein shall it be known... that I and thy people have found grace in thy sight? is it not in that thou goest with us? The witnesses note so shall we be separated, I and thy people, from all the people that are upon the face of the earth.

The witnesses note Moses pleading that God's going with them is the one proof of grace and the very thing that separates them from all peoples — that by his presence and protection they should be saved from idolatry and kept in the true worship; the presence of God among his people the one thing that marks them his and distinguishes them from the world.

So the witnesses make the verse God's presence the one mark and proof of his special favour. The lesson the expositors draw is that God's going with them was the only token of grace and what separated them from all nations, his presence marking them his. The witnesses note the wherein known: "wherein shall it be known" — no other token; the special. The expositors observe the thou goest with us: "in that thou goest with us" — the one mark; the the proof. The witnesses press the separated: "so shall we be separated" — set apart; the from the nations. The expositors draw the lesson on the saved from idolatry: saved from idolatry, preserved in the true worship. The expositors note the peculiar people: his dwelling makes them peculiar. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the so shall we be separated I and thy people from all the people that are upon the face of the earth, and the mark. The verse thus records God's presence the mark of his people — teaching that his going with them was the proof of grace and what separated them from all nations.
17:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, I will do this thing also that thou hast spoken: for thou hast found grace in my sight, and I know thee by name." The witnesses note God granting Moses' request, because he had found grace and was known by name. Gill notes God would do this thing also that Moses asked, namely, go with them himself in that amazing and distinguished manner, in the pillar of cloud and fire. The witnesses note God answering that he would do the thing Moses had asked, for he had found grace and God knew him by name; the full granting of the intercession, God yielding to the importunate plea — that God grants all Moses asked, on the ground of grace: "I will do this thing also that thou hast spoken: for thou hast found grace in my sight, and I know thee by name"; the witnesses note the grant, "I will do this thing also that thou hast spoken" — that God would "go with them himself in that amazing and distinguished manner, in the pillar of cloud and fire," granting the very presence asked; the witnesses note the "also," that this was a further favour beyond the former (v.14), God granting more upon being further entreated; the witnesses note the ground, "for thou hast found grace in my sight, and I know thee by name" — that God grants the request on the ground of Moses' having found grace and being known by name, the same plea Moses had urged; the witnesses note the great encouragement the commentators draw, that importunate intercession prevails with God, and that he answers the prayer of him whom he favours; the witnesses note that God yields to the pleading of his servants, granting the desire of them that fear him; so that God grants all Moses asked, of grace; and I know thee by name. The witnesses note I will do this thing also that thou hast spoken: for thou hast found grace in my sight. The witnesses note and I know thee by name.

The witnesses note God granting all Moses asked — to go with them himself in the pillar of cloud and fire — for he had found grace and God knew him by name; importunate intercession prevailing with God, who answers the prayer of him whom he favours and yields to the pleading of his servants, granting the desire of them that fear him.

So the witnesses make the verse the full granting of the intercession. The lesson the expositors draw is that God yielded to Moses' importunate plea, granting all he asked on the ground of grace, importunate intercession prevailing with God. The witnesses note the I will do this thing: "I will do this thing also" — Gill notes go himself; the I will do this thing; the go himself; the in the pillar; the cloud and fire; the granted. The expositors observe the also: the "also," a further favour; the also; the a further favour; the beyond the former; the more; the entreated. The witnesses press the found grace: "thou hast found grace" — the ground; the found grace; the the ground; the the plea; the urged; the same. The expositors draw the lesson on the know thee by name: "I know thee by name" — favoured; the know thee by name; the favoured; the known; the his servant; the the ground. The expositors note the answers the favoured: answers him whom he favours. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and I know thee by name, and the granting. The verse thus records the full granting of the intercession — teaching that God granted all Moses asked, importunate intercession prevailing with God.
18:
"And he said, I beseech thee, shew me thy glory." The witnesses note Moses beseeching God to show him his glory. Gill notes this was not any visible lustre and brightness Moses had seen, nor the glorious essence of God, which is invisible and cannot be seen, but a fuller discovery of God's grace and goodness. The witnesses note Moses, emboldened by the granting of his former requests, beseeching God to show him his glory; the rising of holy desire to a yet higher favour, the sight of God's glory — that, having gained so much, Moses asks the highest of all: "I beseech thee, shew me thy glory"; the witnesses note the request, "shew me thy glory" — not "any visible lustre... as a symbol of the divine Presence" he had already seen, nor "the glorious essence of God... which is invisible and cannot be seen," of which Moses could not be ignorant; the witnesses note that what he sought was a fuller and clearer discovery of God's grace and goodness, the brightest manifestation his soul could bear; the witnesses note the holy boldness, that, having found grace and gained his suit, Moses presses on to ask the greatest favour, to see God's glory; the witnesses note the lesson the commentators draw, that the more a soul knows of God, the more it longs to know, holy desire ever reaching higher; the witnesses note that to see God's glory is the height of the soul's longing; the witnesses note that grace makes the soul ambitious of more of God; so that Moses asks to see God's glory; shew me thy glory. The witnesses note And he said, I beseech thee. The witnesses note shew me thy glory.

The witnesses note Moses, emboldened by his granted requests, beseeching God to show him his glory — not the invisible essence, but a fuller and clearer discovery of his grace and goodness, the brightest manifestation his soul could bear; the more a soul knows of God, the more it longs to know, holy desire ever reaching higher, grace making the soul ambitious of more of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the rising of holy desire to the sight of God's glory. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses, having gained much, pressed on to ask the highest of all, to see God's glory, the more a soul knows the more it longs to know. The witnesses note the shew me thy glory: "shew me thy glory" — Gill notes not the essence; the shew me thy glory; the not the essence; the not the lustre seen; the a fuller discovery; the his goodness. The expositors observe the the brightest manifestation: the brightest manifestation; the the brightest; the manifestation; the his soul could bear; the of grace; the and goodness. The witnesses press the holy boldness: holy boldness, asking the greatest; the holy boldness; the the greatest favour; the having found grace; the presses on; the his suit. The expositors draw the lesson on the longs to know more: the more it longs to know; the longs to know more; the the more known; the the more longed; the holy desire; the higher. The witnesses observe the the height of longing: the height of the soul's longing; the the height of longing; the the height; the the soul's; the longing; the to see God. The expositors note the ambitious of more: grace makes ambitious of more of God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the shew me thy glory, and the request. The verse thus records the rising of holy desire — teaching that Moses asked to see God's glory, the more a soul knows the more it longs to know.
19:
"And he said, I will make all my goodness pass before thee, and I will proclaim the name of the LORD before thee; and will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and will shew mercy on whom I will shew mercy." The witnesses note God promising to make his goodness pass before Moses, to proclaim his name, and to be sovereign in his grace. Gill notes the glory of the Lord lies in his goodness, which appears in the works of his hands and the methods of his providence, especially in the distribution of his sovereign grace and mercy, and particularly in his pardoning grace. The witnesses note God answering that he would make all his goodness pass before Moses, proclaim his name, and be gracious and merciful to whom he would; the great answer, that God's glory is his goodness, displayed in sovereign grace — that God shows his glory by his goodness and free mercy: "I will make all my goodness pass before thee, and I will proclaim the name of the LORD... and will be gracious to whom I will be gracious"; the witnesses note that "the glory of the LORD lies in his goodness," and so, to show his glory, God would make "all [his] goodness pass before" Moses; the witnesses note the proclaiming, "I will proclaim the name of the LORD before thee" — that God would declare his name and nature, his attributes, chiefly his grace and mercy (fulfilled 34:6-7); the witnesses note the sovereignty, "will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and will shew mercy on whom I will shew mercy" — that God's grace and mercy are free and sovereign, given of his own will to whom he pleases, none having a claim (Rom. 9:15); the witnesses note that the chiefest glory of God is his pardoning grace; so that God shows his glory by his goodness and sovereign mercy; will shew mercy on whom I will shew mercy. The witnesses note I will make all my goodness pass before thee, and I will proclaim the name of the LORD before thee. The witnesses note and will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and will shew mercy on whom I will shew mercy.

The witnesses note God answering that he would make all his goodness pass before Moses and proclaim his name — for the glory of the LORD lies in his goodness — and would be gracious and merciful to whom he would; God's grace and mercy free and sovereign, given of his own will to whom he pleases, none having a claim, the chiefest glory of God his pardoning grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the great answer, that God's glory is his goodness, displayed in sovereign grace. The lesson the expositors draw is that God shows his glory by his goodness and free mercy, sovereign and owing nothing to any, his pardoning grace his chiefest glory. The witnesses note the all my goodness: "all my goodness pass before thee" — Gill notes the glory is goodness; the all my goodness; the the glory is goodness; the his works; the his providence; the his grace. The expositors observe the proclaim the name: "proclaim the name of the LORD" — his nature; the his grace. The witnesses press the sovereign grace: "gracious to whom I will be gracious" — free; the no claim. The expositors draw the lesson on the glory in goodness: glory shines in goodness and free grace. The expositors note the pardoning grace: pardoning grace his chiefest glory. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and will be gracious to whom I will be gracious and will shew mercy on whom I will shew mercy, and the answer. The verse thus records the great answer — teaching that God's glory is his goodness, displayed in sovereign and free mercy.
20:
"And he said, Thou canst not see my face: for there shall no man see me, and live." The witnesses note God declaring that Moses could not see his face, for no man sees him and lives. Gill notes by his face is meant not his essence and very nature, which Moses must know he could never see, but the brightest displays of his grace and goodness in Christ, the fullest discoveries, which are too much for man in the present state to bear. The witnesses note God telling Moses he could not see his face, for no man could see God and live; the limit set to Moses' request, the unbearable fulness of God's glory to mortal man — that the full sight of God is more than flesh can bear: "Thou canst not see my face: for there shall no man see me, and live"; the witnesses note the face, "thou canst not see my face" — meaning not "his essence... his very nature... that Moses must know he could never see," but "the brightest displays of his grace and goodness... the fullest discoveries," which "are too much for man, in the present state... to" bear; the witnesses note the reason, "there shall no man see me, and live" — that the full and immediate sight of God's glory would overwhelm and consume mortal man; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that, while God grants much, he grants according to our measure, the full vision being reserved for the state of glory (1 Cor. 13:12; 1 John 3:2); the witnesses note that man in the flesh cannot bear the unveiled glory of God; the witnesses note that the sight of God face to face is the hope of the life to come, not of this; so that Moses cannot see God's face and live; for there shall no man see me, and live. The witnesses note And he said, Thou canst not see my face. The witnesses note for there shall no man see me, and live.

The witnesses note God setting a limit to Moses' request — he could not see God's face, for no man sees him and lives — not the essence Moses could never see, but the brightest displays of his glory, too much for mortal man in this state to bear; the full vision reserved for the state of glory, the sight of God face to face the hope of the life to come.

So the witnesses make the verse the limit set to Moses' request, the unbearable fulness of God's glory. The lesson the expositors draw is that man in the flesh cannot bear the full sight of God, which would overwhelm him, the full vision reserved for the state of glory. The witnesses note the not see my face: "thou canst not see my face" — Gill notes the brightest displays; the not see my face; the the brightest displays; the not the essence; the in Christ; the the fullest. The expositors observe the too much for man: "too much for man" — to bear in this state. The witnesses press the no man see me and live: "no man see me, and live" — consume; the the full sight. The expositors draw the lesson on the according to our measure: granted according to our measure; the according to our measure; the granted; the our measure; the much; the not all. The expositors note the hope to come: the sight the hope to come, not this life. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for there shall no man see me and live, and the limit. The verse thus records the limit set to the request — teaching that man cannot bear the full sight of God, the full vision reserved for glory.
21:
"And the LORD said, Behold, there is a place by me, and thou shalt stand upon a rock:" The witnesses note God appointing Moses a place near him, to stand upon a rock. Gill notes there was a place by him, near him, upon the rock where it had been for many days, near to which there was a fit place for Moses to have that view of the goodness and glory of God. The witnesses note God appointing Moses a place near him, to stand upon a rock; the providing of a place and a standing for the vision, near God and upon the rock — that God himself sets the place where Moses shall stand: "Behold, there is a place by me, and thou shalt stand upon a rock"; the witnesses note the place, "there is a place by me" — "near him," a place God himself appointed close to his presence, fit for Moses to have his view of the divine glory; the witnesses note the standing, "thou shalt stand upon a rock" — that Moses was to stand "upon the rock" where God's presence had been, a sure and firm standing; the witnesses note the type the commentators draw, that the rock on which Moses stood to see God's glory shadows Christ, the Rock, on whom alone a man may stand to behold the glory and goodness of God; the witnesses note that there is no safe standing to see God but on the Rock; the witnesses note that God appoints both the place and the standing for the soul that would see his glory; so that Moses is set on the rock, near God, to see his glory; and thou shalt stand upon a rock. The witnesses note And the LORD said, Behold, there is a place by me. The witnesses note and thou shalt stand upon a rock.

The witnesses note God appointing Moses a place near him and a standing upon a rock for the vision of his glory — a sure and firm standing; the rock shadowing Christ, the Rock, on whom alone a man may stand to behold the glory and goodness of God, there being no safe standing to see God but on the Rock.

So the witnesses make the verse the providing of a place and standing for the vision. The lesson the expositors draw is that God set Moses near him on the rock, a sure standing, the rock a type of Christ on whom alone a man may stand to see God's glory. The witnesses note the a place by me: "a place by me" — Gill notes near him; the a place by me; the near him; the appointed; the close; the his presence. The expositors observe the upon a rock: "stand upon a rock" — a firm standing; the upon a rock; the a firm standing; the the rock; the sure; the God's presence. The witnesses press the the rock Christ: the rock a type of Christ; the the rock Christ; the the rock; the a type; the of Christ; the the Rock. The expositors draw the lesson on the stand on the Rock: on whom alone a man may stand; the stand on the Rock; the on whom alone; the a man may stand; the to behold; the the glory. The expositors note the appoints the place: God appoints the place and standing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and thou shalt stand upon a rock, and the place. The verse thus records the place and standing — teaching that God set Moses on the rock to see his glory, the rock a type of Christ on whom alone a man may stand.
22:
"And it shall come to pass, while my glory passeth by, that I will put thee in a clift of the rock, and will cover thee with my hand while I pass by:" The witnesses note God promising to put Moses in a cleft of the rock and cover him with his hand while his glory passed by. Charles Ellicott notes, with Kalisch, that the mysteriousness of this obscure section attains its highest climax in the three last verses, human language straining to express the unutterable. The witnesses note God promising to put Moses in a cleft of the rock and cover him with his hand while his glory passed by; the sheltering of Moses in the rock and under God's hand from the overwhelming glory — that God hides his servant while his glory goes by: "while my glory passeth by, that I will put thee in a clift of the rock, and will cover thee with my hand while I pass by"; the witnesses note the passing, "while my glory passeth by" — that "the displays of his grace and goodness are made" in passing; the witnesses note the hiding, "I will put thee in a clift of the rock" — that Moses was sheltered in a cleft of the rock, hidden from the full blaze of the glory; the witnesses note the covering, "will cover thee with my hand while I pass by" — that God's own hand covered Moses, shielding him from what he could not bear; the witnesses note the great type the commentators draw, that the cleft of the rock, in which Moses was hidden and covered, shadows Christ, the cleft Rock, in whom the believer is hidden and sheltered, safe to behold God's glory; the witnesses note the awe the commentators own, that "human language" strains here, the matter being too deep and mysterious for full utterance; the witnesses note that only in the Rock, covered by God's hand, may a man stand before his glory; so that Moses is hidden in the rock under God's hand; while I pass by. The witnesses note while my glory passeth by, that I will put thee in a clift of the rock. The witnesses note and will cover thee with my hand while I pass by.

The witnesses note God hiding Moses in a cleft of the rock and covering him with his hand while his glory passed by — sheltering his servant from what he could not bear; the cleft of the rock shadowing Christ, the cleft Rock, in whom the believer is hidden and sheltered, safe to behold God's glory, only in the Rock and under God's hand may a man stand before his glory.

So the witnesses make the verse the sheltering of Moses in the rock from the overwhelming glory. The lesson the expositors draw is that God hid Moses in the cleft and covered him with his hand, a type of Christ the cleft Rock in whom the believer is hidden, safe to behold God's glory. The witnesses note the glory passeth by: "while my glory passeth by" — Ellicott notes the displays made; the in passing. The expositors observe the clift of the rock: "I will put thee in a clift of the rock" — sheltered; the from the blaze. The witnesses press the cover with my hand: "cover thee with my hand" — shielded; the from what he could not bear. The expositors draw the lesson on the the cleft Rock Christ: the cleft Rock, Christ, in whom we are hidden. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and will cover thee with my hand while I pass by, and the sheltering. The verse thus records the sheltering of Moses — teaching that God hid him in the cleft and covered him, a type of Christ the cleft Rock in whom the believer is hidden.
23:
"And I will take away mine hand, and thou shalt see my back parts: but my face shall not be seen." The witnesses note God promising that Moses should see his back parts, but not his face. Gill notes that as being covered with the hand may signify the obscurity of the former dispensation, the taking of it away may denote a clearer revelation of the grace and goodness of God in Christ under the Gospel, yet what is seen now compared to the future is but the back parts. The witnesses note God promising to take away his hand, that Moses should see his back parts, but his face should not be seen; the partial and gracious vision granted, the back parts and not the face — that God shows what man can bear, not the full glory: "I will take away mine hand, and thou shalt see my back parts: but my face shall not be seen"; the witnesses note the uncovering, "I will take away mine hand" — that, the glory passed, God would remove his covering hand; the witnesses note the sight, "thou shalt see my back parts" — not the full face, but the after-glory, what could be seen and borne; the witnesses note the limit, "my face shall not be seen" — the full, immediate glory still withheld; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the covering hand may signify "the obscurity of the former dispensation," and its taking away "a more clear revelation of the grace and goodness of God in Christ... under the Gospel," yet even this, compared to the glory to come, is but "the back parts"; the witnesses note that all our present sight of God, even the clearest in Christ, is partial, the full vision reserved for heaven (1 Cor. 13:12); the witnesses note that we now see in part, but then face to face; so that Moses sees the back parts, not the face; but my face shall not be seen. The witnesses note And I will take away mine hand, and thou shalt see my back parts. The witnesses note but my face shall not be seen.

The witnesses note God granting Moses to see his back parts but not his face — the partial vision man can bear, not the full glory; the covering hand signifying the obscurity of the former dispensation, its taking away a clearer revelation of God's grace in Christ under the Gospel, yet even this, compared to the glory to come, but the back parts, the full vision reserved for heaven.

So the witnesses make the verse the partial and gracious vision, the back parts and not the face. The lesson the expositors draw is that God showed what man could bear, not the full glory, all our present sight of God partial, the full vision reserved for heaven. The witnesses note the take away mine hand: "take away mine hand" — Gill notes the glory passed; the take away mine hand; the the glory passed; the removed; the the covering; the uncovered. The expositors observe the see my back parts: "thou shalt see my back parts" — the after-glory; the what could be borne. The witnesses press the face not seen: "my face shall not be seen" — withheld; the the full glory. The expositors draw the lesson on the former dispensation: the obscurity of the former; the former dispensation; the the obscurity; the the former; the the hand; the covered. The expositors note the but the back parts: even this but the back parts, compared to the glory to come. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but my face shall not be seen, and the vision. The verse thus records the partial vision — teaching that God showed what man could bear, all our present sight of God partial, the full vision reserved for heaven.
